When calling `any()` with a generator, once a truthy value is encountered, any remaining items from the generator are not processed.

```
>>> def true():
...  print('true')
...  return True
...
>>> def false():
...  print('false')
...  return False
...
>>> any(fn() for fn in (true, false))
true
True
>>> any(fn() for fn in (false, true))
false
true
True
```

So when there is more than one requirements file, if the first requirements file has changed (or is missing), then any subsequent requirements hash files are not built. When using a single tox venv, this results in X venvs being created/recreated where X is the number of requirements file referenced in  tox `deps`.

This is sort of confusing on what is happening here. For this functional test, here is what I recommend:


1) Split these tests out to two test methods: one where the user provides the parameter and one where you inject it for the user. In general if you are making two somewhat unrelated assertions, it is a sign you should break them up into separate tests because if the first assertion fails you will not be able to see if the second assertion fails as well.
2) For the case where you are injecting the idempotency token for the user, I would recommend instead of registering that lambda event, use ``botocore.stub.ANY``
3) For stubbers, feel free to use the context manager to activate and deactivate the stubber.

'dest' is a parameter supported by argparse. If you use 'argparse.SUPPRESS' for dest, then you can have the option in your command signature but NOT have it become part of the namespace, which obviates the need to later suppress the parameter. The only downside is that it means it doesn't end up in our command table... so that's why this little fix was necessary--to get the best of both worlds :)

@bmw, I'm not sure if I understand step `3.`

`_help_output` calls `parse` (where `zope.component.getUtility` gets
mocked (step `2.`)); so, if I'm understanding it correctly, the
mocked `notification` is not available in `_help_output`.

How about this:

```diff
diff --git a/certbot/tests/cli_test.py b/certbot/tests/cli_test.py
index c4f9ad07..d19c15c2 100644
--- a/certbot/tests/cli_test.py
+++ b/certbot/tests/cli_test.py
@@ -2,6 +2,7 @@
 import argparse
 import unittest
 import os
+import sys
 import tempfile
 
 import mock
@@ -51,10 +52,23 @@ class ParseTest(unittest.TestCase):  # pylint: disable=too-many-public-methods
         reload_module(cli)
 
     @staticmethod
-    def parse(*args, **kwargs):
+    def _unmocked_parse(*args, **kwargs):
         """Get result of cli.prepare_and_parse_args."""
         return cli.prepare_and_parse_args(PLUGINS, *args, **kwargs)
 
+    @staticmethod
+    def parse(*args, **kwargs):
+        """Mocks zope.component.getUtility and calls _unmocked_parse."""
+        with test_util.patch_get_utility() as mocked_get_utility:
+            mocked_get_utility().notification.side_effect = \
+              ParseTest._stdout_notification
+            return ParseTest._unmocked_parse(*args, **kwargs)
+
+    @staticmethod
+    def _stdout_notification(*args, **kwargs):
+        """Send notification to stdout."""
+        sys.stdout.write(args[0] if args else kwargs['message'])
+
     def _help_output(self, args):
         "Run a command, and return the output string for scrutiny"
 
@@ -104,8 +118,8 @@ class ParseTest(unittest.TestCase):  # pylint: disable=too-many-public-methods
         self.assertEqual(namespace.chain_path, os.path.abspath(chain))
         self.assertEqual(namespace.fullchain_path, os.path.abspath(fullchain))
 
-    @test_util.patch_get_utility(stdout_notification=True)
-    def test_help(self, unused_mock_get_utility):
+
+    def test_help(self):
         self._help_output(['--help'])  # assert SystemExit is raised here
         out = self._help_output(['--help', 'all'])
```

Please revert that "solution" - by removing the ability to set FPS to 0, you're removing a key feature that has been part of Beholder since Chris wrote it.  It's important both to the UI - it lets you pause the video updates so that you have time to inspect the visualization and section values in detail, which is basically impossible at a 1 second update interval - and also to training performance, since by setting FPS to 0 it tells the actual Beholder code in the model to skip frame generation to reduce overhead during training.  The feature is already documented in [his readme](https://github.com/chrisranderson/beholder/blame/06a0cd4ffbda55e79a46ce98cd82ecc851523038/README.md#L129), [our readme](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blame/70aece3b60800d872cf60cb0f4ab9a5dc1f94ed7/tensorboard/plugins/beholder/README.md#L134-L135), and in [his video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=06HjEr0OX5k&t=114).

We shouldn't take away a valuable feature just because in an ideal world we would reimplement it using websockets (and as a aside, I still think you would want an FPS control for UX reasons as well as to be able to throttle down the generation overhead on the model training side, so I don't actually see how that would change things).

If you're not interested in fixing the code to avoid XHRs when FPS is set to 0, we can leave it as is and I will fix it post-release.

In order to make it better with the Actors, I would implement the following base workflow:
- State machine (SM) receives a message
- SM analyzes whether it can process the message in the current state
- SM processes the message
- SM goes to the new state if needed
- SM performs some actions after moving to the new state.

I think the main difference with the current implementation is that we don't analyse whether we can apply the message (event) before trying to apply it.

I kept it mostly b/c it was in the SO answer and in the pure python description of a `@property`: https://docs.python.org/3/howto/descriptor.html#properties.

So I guess we need to decide what ought to happen if a user tries to access, e.g., `galsim.Gaussian.noise`...  I don't have a strong opinion on this, but returning `None` seems reasonable (though this would also be the return value without this branch I think).

This was an interesting py2 vs py3 issue that I hadn't seen before:
In 2.7, `functools.wraps()` doesn't fix the signature of `wrapper()` to match the original function.
As a result, `test.ios.test_getters.TestGetter.test_method_signatures()` fails. Since that test skips private methods the easiest way to work-around was to decorate a private method and call it from the public one.
If you're aware of a more elegant fix then I'm happy to incorporate that?
